title: Privileged Access — Sudo and RunAs Usage Outside PAM Session

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.8.2 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.8.2 (Privileged access rights) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.26: Privileged Access — Sudo and RunAs Usage Outside PAM Session.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.26' running on linux:audit type=USER_CMD, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001:2013 Annex A.12.4.3 Administrator and operator logs — UC is the administrator/operator activity log (sudo and RunAs) detection required by the 2013 control.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO/IEC 27001 A.12.4.3 (Administrator and operator logs (2013)) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.26: Privileged Access — Sudo and RunAs Usage Outside PAM Session.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.26' running on linux:audit type=USER_CMD, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=os sourcetype=linux:audit type=USER_CMD earliest=-24h
| where match(exe,"(?i)sudo|su|pkexec")
| lookup pam_sessions.csv user, _time OUTPUT pam_session_id
| where isnull(pam_session_id)
description: Surfaces local privilege elevation without corresponding PAM session ID for privileged access monitoring.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001, ISO/IEC 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes os and sourcetypes linux:audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Clock sync with PAM; (2) break-glass accounts excluded; (3) monthly privileged user review. Indexes required: `index=os`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=linux:audit`; Lookups: `pam_sessions`, `pam_sessions.csv`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src span=1d | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Authentication data model (Authentication.Authentication).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Scheduled maintenance window access** — Administrative access during documented maintenance windows with change tickets is pre-approved operational activity.

2. **Automated service account patterns** — Service accounts performing scheduled tasks generate high-frequency access patterns that are normal operational behaviour per the automation register.

3. **Break-glass emergency access** — Emergency access during declared incidents with post-incident review and documented justification follows the approved escalation process.

4. **Access review cycle timing** — Users retaining access between scheduled access review dates are within the approved recertification cadence per the access governance policy.

5. **Temporary elevated privileges** — Time-bounded privilege escalation for approved projects or maintenance activities with documented start and end dates.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (Splunkbase app ID 833) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `os` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `A.8.2`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO 27001 A.8.2 (Privileged access rights) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.26: Privileged Access — Sudo and RunAs Usage Outside PAM Session." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (Splunkbase app ID 833), landing events in `os` under `linux:audit`. Scripted inputs at hourly cadence produce ~50-200 events/host/hour (~100-400 MB/host/month). Auditd log volume adds 500 MB - 2 GB/host/month.

Configure the input through Deployment Server > Forwarder Management > Splunk_TA_nix serverclass. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[script://./bin/openPorts.sh]
disabled = 0
interval = 3600
sourcetype = linux:network_listeners
index = linux
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `host, proto, port, process, pid, user, cmdline`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=os earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Clock sync with PAM. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** break-glass accounts excluded. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 3:** monthly privileged user review. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `count()` across the last 24 hours, grouped by `the analytical dimensions`. For "Privileged Access — Sudo and RunAs Usage Outside PAM Session," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative. The aggregation approach is chosen to produce one result row per analytical unit, which is the typical requirement for compliance dashboarding and report generation.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `the analytical dimensions` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(the analytical dimensions) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src span=1d | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.26: Privileged Access — Sudo and RunAs Usage Outside PAM Session]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.26,reg=ISO_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=os sourcetype=linux:audit earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.26: ISO 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (the key fields):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Time trend (the key fields):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Privileged Access — Sudo and RunAs Usage Outside PAM Session. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Grant a test user elevated access to a controlled system without a corresponding approval record in the access management system; verify the monitoring search detects the unapproved privilege within 1 hour and generates an alert with user, system, and missing approval details.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Grant a test user access following the full approval workflow with manager sign-off, risk assessment, and time-limited duration recorded; verify no access violation alert fires and the access appears as properly authorised in the dashboard.

evidence: Access governance report showing recertification completion rates, privilege changes, approval chain documentation, and anomalous access investigation outcomes.
exclusions: Approved maintenance window access; automated service account operations per register; break-glass access during declared incidents; access within recertification cycle timing; project-based temporary elevation.
